title: From Control To Surrender
study: A Study In The Life Of King David
scriptureForWeek: Make sure to pay attention closely to the daily reading because they jump several places to recap the life of King David.
weekTheme: Boys grasp, Men trust God will remember us What do you want to be remembered for? This isn’t something boys tend to spend much time really thinking through, but it's an important question every man needs to ask himself. What we are remembered for is called our legacy. A legacy is what you are known for after the period of time has gone, both for good and for bad. You may not be aware of it right now, but you are leaving a legacy as we speak. You will be remembered for what you have done in this season of life, for the rest of your life. Kind of wild hey? When people normally think about legacy, they tend to think of famous people. They think of LeBron James and his basketball accomplishments or Warren Buffet and his wealth, but a man’s true legacy is actually much wider than that. It’s not just the big stuff we do that is remembered; it’s all parts of our life that matter. Things like how we treat people, how we did in school, how we handle hard circumstances, how we forgive people who hurt us. It all counts. And it’s important to remember the audience that matters most. It’s possible to be really successful in the eyes of the world and to be a failure in the sight of God. And it’s possible to be rejected by the world but have an amazing legacy in the kingdom of heaven. As a believer, David surrendered his legacy to God. He knew that he had done some amazing things that honored God and some tragic things that didn’t, but he left how he would be remembered in God's hand. This week you are going to close out the 4th shift in the Primal Path by looking at the legacy left by David. There are a ton of important lessons in here, so let’s jump in.

days.title: The Charge
days.scripture: 1 KINGS 2:1-12 As the time of King David’s death approached, he gave this charge to his son Solomon: “I am going where everyone on earth must someday go. Take courage and be a man. Observe the requirements of the LORD your God, and follow all his ways. Keep the decrees, commands, regulations, and laws written in the Law of Moses so that you will be successful in all you do and wherever you go. If you do this, then the LORD will keep the promise he made to me. He told me, ‘If your descendants live as they should and follow me faithfully with all their heart and soul, one of them will always sit on the throne of Israel.’ “And there is something else. You know what Joab son of Zeruiah did to me when he murdered my two army commanders, Abner son of Ner and Amasa son of Jether. He pretended that it was an act of war, but it was done in a time of peace, staining his belt and sandals with innocent blood. Do with him what you think best, but don’t let him grow old and go to his grave in peace. “Be kind to the sons of Barzillai of Gilead. Make them permanent guests at your table, for they took care of me when I fled from your brother Absalom. “And remember Shimei son of Gera, the man from Bahurim in Benjamin. He cursed me with a terrible curse as I was fleeing to Mahanaim. When he came down to meet me at the Jordan River, I swore by the LORD that I would not kill him. But that oath does not make him innocent. You are a wise man, and you will know how to arrange a bloody death for him.” Then David died and was buried with his ancestors in the City of David. David had reigned over Israel for forty years, seven of them in Hebron and thirty-three in Jerusalem. Solomon became king and sat on the throne of David his father, and his kingdom was firmly established.
days.reflection: There is a scene in most good movies called “the death scene.” This is that powerful and tragic moment when one of the beloved characters realizes they are going to die. The music gets soft and emotional, and the cameras come in close to capture the final words. You hope the person says what needs to be said. Words like “I am sorry, I love you, keep going, or you can make it.” Stuff like that. Those final words are designed to give encouragement and closure to the people around them so they can keep going. What a man says right before he dies are some of his most important words. A man on his deathbed has reflected on his life and is trying to convey only the most essential things. In an incredible scene from today’s reading, we actually get to hear some of David’s last words. David calls his son Solomon to him to give him some important advice on how to become the king and take over his father's kingdom, and Solomon is going to inherit a lot. A city, a treasury, hundreds of thousands of people to lead, officials, territory, and strategic relationships. Solomon is going to have a ton of stuff on his hands from day one. David knows this and gives three key commands. Command 1. COURAGE Take courage and be a man. Leadership is hard. It is not for the faint of heart. David knew Solomon would need courage, compassion, and conviction to do his job properly. David called his son to step up. He called him to act as a man, not a boy. Men lead; boys shrink back in fear. Command 2. COMMANDS Observe the requirements of the LORD your God, and follow all his ways. Keep the decrees, commands, regulations, and laws written in the Law of Moses so that you will be successful in all you do and wherever you go. David charged Solomon to be a godly man. To make sure that his leadership was based on what God wanted, not what he wanted. Kings were prone to abuse their power and act like gods. David knew this firsthand because of the sin he had committed with Solomon's mom Bathsheba. So he urged Solomon to be loyal to God and not let power distort or corrupt him. Command 3. CLOSURE Punish Joab. Be kind to Barzillai’s family. Rebuke Shimei. All men live complicated lives with relationships and problems that can last longer than a single life. David asked his son to wrap up some unfinished business that he wasn’t able to get to during his time. Be kind to some people and pay others back. Solomon had a legacy to continue that started before he was born. You too are continuing your family legacy. The reason you are being trained up in the Primal Path is because your family wants you to do well. They want you to become a strong and courageous man. They want you to be a faithful and godly man. They want you to carry on your family legacy. May David's words echo in your ears and his vision inspire your heart. Because we need more men like him in our world today.
questions: If you could go back in time and give some advice to yourself when you were 7 years old, what would you say to yourself?
Do you have any idea what lessons you may want to leave to your own son someday if you have one?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share some lessons that were shared with them from people they respect.

thoughts.verse: A wise person thinks a lot about death, while a fool thinks only about having a good time.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to thank God for how much wisdom he has given you already at this stage of your life. Ask him to give you a vision of leaving a godly legacy behind you.

days.title: Final Words
days.scripture: 2 SAMUEL 23:1-7 These are the last words of David: “David, the son of Jesse, speaks— David, the man who was raised up so high, David, the man anointed by the God of Jacob, David, the sweet psalmist of Israel. “The Spirit of the LORD speaks through me; his words are upon my tongue. The God of Israel spoke. The Rock of Israel said to me: ‘The one who rules righteously, who rules in the fear of God, is like the light of morning at sunrise, like a morning without clouds, like the gleaming of the sun on new grass after rain.’ “Is it not my family God has chosen? Yes, he has made an everlasting covenant with me. His agreement is arranged and guaranteed in every detail. He will ensure my safety and success. But the godless are like thorns to be thrown away, for they tear the hand that touches them. One must use iron tools to chop them down; they will be totally consumed by fire.”
days.reflection: For the last 10 weeks, you have looked at the amazing life of King David. A warrior, poet, king, and lover. He was a man among men and one of the most important men in the Bible. His life was so significant that Jesus himself was born in the family lineage of King David and was known as the “Son of David.” And believe it or not, today you just read King David’s last words. These are the last things he ever spoke. They contain a really important theme, the legacy of the godly and the legacy of the wicked. You are going to leave a legacy no matter what, but you get to determine if it’s going to be a godly or sinful one. A godly legacy will bless everyone who comes behind you. A sinful one will beat down everyone who comes behind you. Here is an example from American History that Larry Ballard found showing a godly and a sinful legacy. Don’t zone out as you read this; it’s actually kind of wild. “Jonathan Edwards, was a Puritan Preacher in the 1700s. He was one of the most respected preachers in his day. He attended Yale at the age of thirteen and later went on to become the president of Princeton college. He married his wife Sara in 1727 and they were blessed with eleven children. Every night when Mr. Edwards was home, he would spend an hour conversing with his family and then praying a blessing over each child. Jonathan and his wife Sarah passed on a great, godly legacy to their eleven children. Jonathan Edwards’ legacy includes: 1 U.S. Vice-President, 1 Dean of a law school, 1 dean of a medical school, 3 U.S. Senators, 3 governors, 3 mayors, 13 college presidents, 30 judges, 60 doctors, 65 professors, 75 Military officers, 80 public office holders, 100 lawyers, 100 clergymen, and 285 college graduates. How may this be explained? Edwards was a godly man, but he was also hard working, intelligent and moral. Furthermore, Winship states, “Much of the capacity and talent, intensity and character of the more than 1,400 of Edwards’ family is due to Mrs. Edwards.” There is another man from around Jonathan Edwards’ time who left a legacy, but this was a sinful one. His name was Max Jukes. “Max Jukes’ legacy came to people’s attention when the family trees of 42 different men in the New York prison system were traced back to him. He lived in New York at about the same period as Edwards. The Jukes family originally was studied by sociologist Richard L. Dugdale in 1877. Jukes’ descendants included: 7 murderers, 60 thieves, 190 prostitutes, 150 other convicts, 310 paupers, and 440 who were physically wrecked by addiction to alcohol. Of the 1,200 descendants that were studied, 300 died prematurely.” You may just feel like a normal guy, thinking about yourself and not worrying about who will come behind you, but you will leave a legacy. You are going to have a much larger impact than you are aware of. And you are already a part of a godly legacy. The fact that someone is taking you through the Primal Path shows you are part of a story of redemption with something great to continue. Keep going, and keep learning. God saw David’s heart and called him when he was a guy around your age. If he used David, he can use you too.
questions: If you were to die right now, what are 3 things that you would like to be remembered for and three things you hope everyone forgets?
Can you think of a scene from a movie that tells the importance and power of a man’s legacy? How what he does goes on to affect another generation?
Ask the person you are doing this study with 3 things they would like to be remembered for if they died. And what are 3 things they wish everyone would forget or forgive?

thoughts.verse: But the love of the LORD remains forever with those who fear him. His salvation extends to the children’s children of those who are faithful to his covenant, of those who obey his commandments! Psalms 103:17-18 Read through his verse slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you in it? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean? Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to thank God that he has given you someone who loves you enough to lay a foundation for a godly legacy in your life.

days.title: The Cost Of Legacy
days.scripture: 2 SAMUEL 24:18-25 That day Gad came to David and said to him, “Go up and build an altar to the LORD on the threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite.” So David went up to do what the LORD had commanded him. When Araunah saw the king and his men coming toward him, he came and bowed before the king with his face to the ground. “Why have you come, my lord the king?” Araunah asked. David replied, “I have come to buy your threshing floor and to build an altar to the LORD there, so that he will stop the plague.” “Take it, my lord the king, and use it as you wish,” Araunah said to David. “Here are oxen for the burnt offering, and you can use the threshing boards and ox yokes for wood to build a fire on the altar. I will give it all to you, Your Majesty, and may the LORD your God accept your sacrifice.” But the king replied to Araunah, “No, I insist on buying it, for I will not present burnt offerings to the LORD my God that have cost me nothing.” So David paid him fifty pieces of silver for the threshing floor and the oxen. David built an altar there to the LORD and sacrificed burnt offerings and peace offerings. And the LORD answered his prayer for the land, and the plague on Israel was stopped.
days.reflection: A good legacy will cost you something. You are not going to be able to drift through life taking the easiest route possible and live a life of consequence. Sacrifice is the price of true greatness. This is true in every area of life. If you want to be good at football, you have to sacrifice time playing video games and watching Netflix to practice your skills. If you want good grades to get into a good college when you are older, you will have to sacrifice some of your free time to study. And it’s the same thing in our life with God. If we want to know God and see his power really move in our lives, it will cost us something. We all sacrifice for what we love. If a guy your age starts liking a girl, he will sacrifice anything to get around her. He will give up sleep, time with the boys, and even some of his favorite hobbies. Love makes you sacrifice for the object of your affection. King David knew that God was worthy of his sacrifice. He didn’t want to offer something to God that another man gave to him simply because he was the king. David wanted God to know that he loved him and thought he was worth the cost. So he paid for the land and built an altar to show God how serious he was. Now we need to be clear here. He wasn’t doing this to earn God's love or to try and make God like him more. He was doing this to express his love and show God that he thought he was worth it. David teaches us that legacies are built on sacrifice, and sacrifices are made from love. And believe it or not, your salvation is the result of someone loving you so much that they paid a personal cost for you. Jesus loved you so much that he was willing to sacrifice himself for you. He thought that you were worth going through the hell of a violent death on the cross for. He could have just stayed in heaven, doing his own thing and remaining unmoved by your problems and sin. But he was willing to sacrifice himself for you because he wanted you to know how much you were loved. The reason Jesus has the greatest legacy in history is because of the cost of the sacrifice he made and the power of the love he showed. David's legacy was defined by sacrifice, and yours will be too. You can surrender trying to be great in the ways the world normally says. Being famous, getting rich, getting a hot wife. All that stuff is temporary. But sacrifices we make for others will always remain, and that is the most important stuff in life.
questions: Can you think of a big sacrifice you made for someone? What was it, and how did you feel?
Can you think of a few ways that people have made sacrifices for you? What are they, and how does it make you feel to know someone is willing to do this for you?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share how they came to believe that Jesus’ sacrifice for their sin and salvation is the most important one of all.

thoughts.verse: This is my commandment: Love each other in the same way I have loved you. There is no greater love than to lay down one’s life for one’s friends. John 15:12-13 Read through his verse slowly a second time. Is there something that stands out to you in it? Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean? Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to thank God that he loved you so much that he gave his only son so that you may have eternal life and not perish.

days.title: It'S The Heart That Counts
days.scripture: ACTS 13:22 But God removed Saul and replaced him with David, a man about whom God said, ‘I have found David son of Jesse, a man after my own heart. He will do everything I want him to do.’
days.reflection: What metrics do we use to measure if someone is successful in our world today? Is it how much money they have or how much fame or attention they get? How many followers they have on social media or if they have a really popular channel on YouTube? People today tend to regard others as successful if they accomplish or accumulate a lot of things. You will remember we talked about this when God chose David in the first place. Do you remember what God said to Samuel in 1 Samuel 16:7? “But the LORD said to Samuel, 'Don’t judge by his appearance or height, for I have rejected him. The LORD doesn’t see things the way you see them. People judge by outward appearance, but the LORD looks at the heart.’” David lived a crazy life. He was a shepherd, a warrior, a singer/songwriter, an outlaw, a king, a fugitive, a husband, and a father. He conquered giants, wrote songs we still sing today, and had to pretend to be crazy to save his own life. He committed adultery, abused his power, and murdered a godly man. He brought the Ark of the Covenant into the city and set up a worship and prayer meeting that lasted 24/7/365 for over 30 years. He paid for most of this out of his own money. David lived an amazing, tragic, adventurous, remarkable, impossible life. He had the highest highs and the lowest lows. He went from the sheep pen to the palace, from hiding to ruling, and yet… and yet… he isn’t remembered for any of these great accomplishments or failures. He is remembered for the condition of his heart. In the book of Acts, when they summarized the whole life of David down to a sentence it said this: But God removed Saul and replaced him with David, a man about whom God said, ‘I have found David son of Jesse, a man after my own heart. He will do everything I want him to do.’ David didn’t just have a good heart, or a clean heart, or a kind heart. David had a heart after God's own heart. There is no higher compliment than that. David was remembered for wanting what God wanted, caring about what God cared about, and loving what God loved. David didn’t get everything right, but he got the most important things right. His heart was after God's own heart. When you think about the kind of legacy you may leave someday, don’t just think about how much money, attention, or accomplishments you leave behind. Leave behind a godly heart. Leave behind a life that sought to know God, sought to please him, and sought to bring him joy. That is the most important thing. You may make mistakes, even some big ones, but they don’t have to define you. Let your heart be your legacy. Guard your heart, protect your heart, grow your heart. In the end, it’s the thing that matters most.
questions: Of all the people you know, who do you think really has a “heart for God?” Why do you think that?
What are a couple of ways that you can think of to cultivate a heart for God?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a time they first began to really get a heart for God. What was that like and how did it happen? How did it change things for them?

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. You do not desire a sacrifice, or I would offer one. You do not want a burnt offering. The sacrifice you desire is a broken spirit. You will not reject a broken and repentant heart, O God.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in any of them?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you a heart for him. This may be kind of a weird prayer, but it’s one he loves to answer.

days.title: Whose Will? His Will!
days.scripture: ACTS 13:36 After David had done the will of God in his own generation, he died and was buried with his ancestors, and his body decayed.
days.reflection: C.S. Lewis (who by now you should know a little bit about - the Narnia guy) has a really amazing quote about heaven and hell. Check it out. There are only two kinds of people in the end: those who say to God, "Thy will be done," and those to whom God says, in the end, "Thy will be done." All that are in Hell, choose it. Without that self-choice there could be no Hell. No soul that seriously and constantly desires joy will ever miss it. Those who seek find. Those who knock it is opened.” This may seem a little cryptic, so you may want to read it again slowly. Lewis is getting at something really interesting here about what will determine the destiny and outcome of a guy's life, and that thing is the contest of the wills. Your will is the part of you that chooses and decides. It's like the steering wheel of your life. Your will is the part of you that determines what it wants and what you are willing to do to get what you want. Every man has to choose whose will will drive a man's life. His will or God's will. Because of sin, our wills are distorted toward sin and rebellion against God. But it doesn’t mean we can't make meaningful choices. God is so kind that he will very often let you get your way. Even if you sin, rebel against him, and turn your back on him, he will let you do that. God doesn’t want robots for his children; he wants those who are willing to surrender their will to him to do what he wants. One of your enemy’s greatest plans for you is to get you to believe that God's will for you is boring and lame and that it will cause you to miss out on all the good stuff in life. But that’s not true. God’s will leads to safety, joy, and delight. Romans 12:2 puts it this way. Don’t copy the behavior and customs of this world, but let God transform you into a new person by changing the way you think. Then you will learn to know God’s will for you, which is good and pleasing and perfect. God's will is GOOD, PLEASING, AND PERFECT. I don’t think most guys your age are aware of that. That’s why Jesus could pray, “Not my will, but yours be done” when he was in the garden getting ready to go to the cross and die for the sins of the world. He knew the cross would be horrific, but there was no other way to save us from our sin so he joyfully went to the cross and surrendered his will. This is what made David such a great man. Yes, he screwed up in some places, but his heart was set on doing God's will as the vision of his life. He wanted to please God and not himself. And because he surrendered his will to God, so much good came through and to him. God established Jerusalem as the center of his presence on earth. David established a kingdom that lead to the coming of Jesus, and David restored the presence of God back in the middle of the people. David’s greatest legacy was getting God's will done through his life. You will have to determine in your life whose will is going to be most important for you: yours or God's. God is so kind that he will let you choose your way. But David teaches us that our own way leads to death. Choose life! Choose to surrender yourself to him. Invite him into all the parts of your life. Who you date, where you go to college, how you deal with conflict with your friends. Seek his will, seek his wisdom, resolve even now that you as a young man will become a man after God's own heart. Control or surrender, it’s up to you. Choose wisely.
questions: Do you think that C.S. Lewis quote is fair or not? Do you think God lets people choose to go to hell? Why would anyone ever do that?
Are there any areas of your life where you are struggling to do what God wants? Would you be willing to share about it?

Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a time they disobeyed God and did their own will. What was it and how did it turn out? Then ask them about a time they surrendered their will to God and what happened as a result.

thoughts.verse: Read through these verses slowly. Do not love this world nor the things it offers you, for when you love the world, you do not have the love of the Father in you. For the world offers only a craving for physical pleasure, a craving for everything we see, and pride in our achievements and possessions. These are not from the Father, but are from this world. And this world is fading away, along with everything that people crave. But anyone who does what pleases God will live forever.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in any of them?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to surrender your will to God. Ask him for vision to see the good, pleasing, and perfect things he has in store for you.
